content.text: A rollback drill is a structured exercise that validates whether a rollback procedure works as documented under realistic conditions. Drills uncover broken assumptions: expired credentials, renamed Kubernetes contexts, changed API endpoints, and commands that require knowledge not written in the runbook. Running drills regularly converts rollback plans from aspirational documents into proven operational procedures.

The goal of a drill is not to succeed — it is to find gaps. A drill that succeeds without surfacing any issues is a drill that did not probe hard enough. Track gap count per drill; a declining gap count over time is a health signal.

This sub-subtopic covers drill design principles, the five-phase drill cycle, scenario library for Claude API services, drill execution scripts, TTR measurement, gap classification, runbook update processes, and how to automate drill scheduling.

attrs.alt: Rollback drill cycle: Plan → Inject Fault → Execute Rollback → Verify → Debrief → repeat quarterly

content.text: A well-designed drill produces evidence about the rollback procedure's reliability. A poorly designed drill produces a false sense of confidence.
Run drills in staging against the actual Kubernetes cluster, Helm releases, and database — not mock scripts
Senior drills don't announce which service will fail; the on-call engineer must diagnose and choose the correct runbook
No shortcuts ('I know what this does'). Every step executed as written. Deviations become runbook updates.
The engineer who runs the drill should not be the one who wrote the runbook. Fresh eyes find gaps.
TTR clock starts at fault injection
Not when the engineer opens the runbook. The clock models a real incident.
All gaps documented before declaring success
A drill is not done when rollback completes — it is done when the debrief log is filed.
Maintain a library of pre-defined drill scenarios, each specifying the fault to inject, the expected runbook to use, and the success criteria.
Manually advance a canary rollout to 10%; then set the error rate metric to exceed P0 threshold by patching the Prometheus recording rule
Error rate returns to baseline; kubectl argo rollouts get shows 'Healthy' on stable revision; smoke test returns HTTP 200
Engineer searches for kubectl context name; rollback command produces unexpected output; smoke test fails after rollback

# Scenario 1: inject fault by setting a synthetic high-error-rate label on canary pods
# (staging only — never inject faults on production canary pods)

# Step 1: Start a canary rollout in staging
kubectl argo rollouts set image claude-gateway \
  gateway=claude-gateway:canary-test -n claude-api-staging

# Step 2: Inject fault — patch a test counter to trigger the alert
kubectl exec -n claude-api-staging deployment/fault-injector -- \
  /bin/sh -c "echo 'inject_error_rate{version="canary"} 0.02' | curl --data-binary @- http://pushgateway:9091/metrics/job/drill"

# Step 3: Confirm alert fires in Alertmanager (drill clock starts here)
watch -n 5 'curl -s http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/alerts | jq "[.[] | select(.labels.action=="rollback")] | length"'
# Wait for count > 0, then hand off to drill participant to execute runbook
Deploy a canary Helm release with a deliberately misconfigured resource limit that causes pod OOMKill
< 3 minutes from OOMKill alert to all pods running previous version
helm history shows rollback revision is active; all pods Running; inference smoke test returns correct response
Engineer does not know target revision number; helm rollback command requires confirmation not mentioned in runbook

# Scenario 2: deploy a misconfigured release (too-low memory limit)
helm upgrade claude-serving ./charts/claude-serving \
  -n claude-serving-staging \
  --set resources.limits.memory=128Mi \   # intentionally too low
  --set image.tag=current-stable            # same image, different limits

# Wait for OOMKill events
kubectl get events -n claude-serving-staging \
  --field-selector reason=OOMKilling --watch
# Drill participant takes over when OOMKill event appears
Enable a test feature flag that injects a 200ms artificial delay; observe P99 latency threshold breach
< 30 seconds from flag disable to latency normalization
Flag API confirms on=false; P99 latency returns to baseline within 35 seconds (30s flag propagation + 5s measurement)
Engineer does not have LaunchDarkly API key in environment; flag disable script not executable
Upload a test system prompt with deliberately degraded instructions; observe eval score regression in monitoring
< 5 minutes from detection to previous template active
aws s3api list-object-versions confirms current version is the reverted one; eval score recovers in next eval run
Engineer doesn't know S3 bucket name; version ID not recorded; CDN invalidation step missing from runbook

Time-to-Recovery is the single quantitative output of every drill. It measures how long the rollback procedure takes from the moment the trigger condition is observable to the moment recovery is verified.
Clock starts when the triggering condition is first observable (alert fires, OOMKill event appears, or the drill facilitator says 'go')
Clock stops when the verification step passes (smoke test returns 200, metrics return to baseline, or runbook verification criteria are met)
Pause time (engineer looking up documentation, waiting for access) counts toward TTR — this is the point
TTR is measured per runbook execution, not per engineer — multiple engineers can collaborate

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# ttr_timer.py — CLI stopwatch for drill TTR measurement
import time, sys, json
from datetime import datetime

def run_ttr_timer(scenario: str, engineer: str):
    print(f"Drill: {scenario}")
    print(f"Engineer: {engineer}")
    input("Press ENTER when fault injection is complete (clock starts)...")

    start = time.time()
    start_ts = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    print(f"Clock started at {start_ts}")
    print("Execute rollback runbook now. Press ENTER when verification passes...")

    input()
    end = time.time()
    end_ts = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    ttr_seconds = end - start

    result = {
        "scenario": scenario,
        "engineer": engineer,
        "start": start_ts,
        "end": end_ts,
        "ttr_seconds": round(ttr_seconds, 1),
        "ttr_formatted": f"{int(ttr_seconds // 60)}m {int(ttr_seconds % 60)}s",
    }
    print(f"\nTTR: {result['ttr_formatted']} ({result['ttr_seconds']}s)")

    with open("drill-results.jsonl", "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(result) + "\n")
    print(f"Result written to drill-results.jsonl")
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    scenario = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else input("Scenario name: ")
    engineer = sys.argv[2] if len(sys.argv) > 2 else input("Engineer name: ")
    run_ttr_timer(scenario, engineer)

# ttr_trend.py — analyze TTR trends across drill history
import json
from collections import defaultdict

def load_drill_results(path: str = "drill-results.jsonl") -> list:
    results = []
    with open(path) as f:
        for line in f:
            results.append(json.loads(line.strip()))
    return results

def analyze_ttr_by_scenario(results: list):
    by_scenario = defaultdict(list)
    for r in results:
        by_scenario[r["scenario"]].append(r["ttr_seconds"])

    print(f"{'Scenario':<45} {'Runs':>5} {'Avg TTR':>10} {'Last TTR':>10} {'Target':>8}")
    print("-" * 80)

    targets = {
        "api-gateway-canary-rollback": 60,
        "model-serving-helm-rollback": 180,
        "feature-flag-kill-switch": 30,
        "prompt-template-revert": 300,
        "db-schema-rollback": 1800,
    }

    for scenario, ttrs in sorted(by_scenario.items()):
        avg = sum(ttrs) / len(ttrs)
        last = ttrs[-1]
        target = targets.get(scenario, float("inf"))
        status = "OK" if last <= target else "MISS"
        print(f"{scenario:<45} {len(ttrs):>5} {avg:>9.0f}s {last:>9.0f}s {target:>7}s {status}")

results = load_drill_results()
analyze_ttr_by_scenario(results)
Every gap found during a drill is classified by type and assigned a resolution before the drill is closed. Unclassified gaps become orphaned observations that nobody fixes.
Engineer lacks kubectl context or IAM role needed by runbook
Runbook step references a command with outdated flag syntax
Required CLI tool not installed in on-call engineer's environment
Runbook doesn't state who has authority to approve rollback
TTR exceeded target; rollback command slower than expected at scale
Engineer did not know which runbook to use for the observed symptoms

# drill-debrief-20240410.yaml
drill:
  date: "2024-04-10"
  scenario: api-gateway-canary-rollback
  engineer: alice@
  facilitator: bob@
  ttr_seconds: 94
  ttr_target_seconds: 60
  ttr_status: MISS

gaps_found:
  - id: GAP-001
    type: access
    description: "Engineer's kubeconfig did not include claude-api-staging context. Spent 35s locating correct context."
    root_cause: "Onboarding checklist does not include staging kubeconfig setup."
    resolution: "Add kubeconfig setup to on-call onboarding checklist."
    owner: platform-team@
    due_date: "2024-04-17"
    status: open

  - id: GAP-002
    type: documentation
    description: "Step 3 says 'watch the rollout status' but does not include the --timeout flag, causing engineer to wait indefinitely."
    root_cause: "Runbook was written before --timeout flag was added to kubectl argo rollouts."
    resolution: "Add --timeout 120s to step 3 rollback command."
    owner: alice@
    due_date: "2024-04-10"
    status: resolved

runbook_updates_required:
  - file: runbooks/api-gateway-rollback.md
    section: "Step 3"
    change: "Add --timeout 120s to kubectl argo rollouts status command"

next_drill_date: "2024-05-10"

Drills that depend on engineers to schedule them consistently fail to happen. Automated scheduling ensures drills run on cadence and sends reminders that include the scenario assignment and runbook link.

# drill_scheduler.py — schedule and assign rollback drills
import yaml, random
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

DRILL_SCHEDULE = {
    "api-gateway-canary-rollback": {"cadence_days": 30, "env": "staging"},
    "model-serving-helm-rollback": {"cadence_days": 90, "env": "staging"},
    "feature-flag-kill-switch": {"cadence_days": 14, "env": "production-test-flag"},
    "prompt-template-revert": {"cadence_days": 30, "env": "staging"},
    "db-schema-rollback": {"cadence_days": 90, "env": "prod-clone"},
}

def get_next_drill_assignments(on_call_rotation: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
    assignments = []
    today = datetime.utcnow().date()
    for scenario, config in DRILL_SCHEDULE.items():
        last_run = get_last_drill_date(scenario)
        next_due = last_run + timedelta(days=config["cadence_days"])
        if next_due <= today + timedelta(days=7):   # due within 1 week
            engineer = random.choice(on_call_rotation)
            assignments.append({
                "scenario": scenario,
                "due_date": next_due.isoformat(),
                "engineer": engineer,
                "env": config["env"],
                "runbook_url": f"https://wiki/runbooks/{scenario}",
            })
    return assignments

def send_drill_notifications(assignments: list[dict]):
    for a in assignments:
        slack_post(
            channel="#rollback-drills",
            text=(
                f"*Drill Due:* `{a['scenario']}`\n"
                f"*Assigned:* {a['engineer']}\n"
                f"*Due:* {a['due_date']}\n"
                f"*Runbook:* {a['runbook_url']}\n"
                f"*Environment:* {a['env']}"
            )
        )

# .github/workflows/drill-reminder.yml
name: Rollback Drill Reminder
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * 1'   # every Monday 9am UTC

jobs:
  check-drill-schedule:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check overdue drills
        run: |
          python3 scripts/drill_scheduler.py --check-overdue \
            --roster on-call-roster.yaml \
            --notify-slack \
            --slack-token ${{ secrets.SLACK_BOT_TOKEN }}
Not every team needs the same drill complexity. Use the maturity model to progress incrementally rather than starting with the hardest scenarios.
Read the runbook aloud; talk through each step; identify what's missing without executing
Facilitator announces fault; engineer executes runbook with facilitator available for questions
Engineer receives only the alert; no facilitator help; must diagnose, select runbook, execute
Real production traffic; limited-blast-radius fault (test feature flag, synthetic alert); full TTR measurement
Validate TTR meets target under real load
Automated fault injection at random times; on-call must respond without advance notice
Start at Level 1 for any new service or new team member. Move to Level 3 before a major release. Reserve Level 4 for services with mature runbooks and a demonstrated gap-free Level 3 history.

Rollback drills transform runbooks from documents into proven procedures. The five-phase cycle — Plan, Inject Fault, Execute, Verify, Debrief — produces evidence about procedure reliability, access gaps, and knowledge gaps. Every gap is classified and assigned a resolution owner before the drill closes. TTR is measured for every drill, trended over time, and compared against targets per service. Automate drill scheduling to prevent cadence drift. Progress through maturity levels — tabletop first, guided execution second, unguided third — before attempting production drills. A team that drills quarterly has a rollback capability that is ready to execute at 3am after being paged from sleep.
Drill goal is to find gaps, not to succeed — a drill with zero gaps did not probe hard enough
TTR clock starts at fault injection (not when runbook opens); pause and access time counts
Gap types: access, documentation, tool, process, performance, knowledge — each has a resolution owner
Automate drill scheduling (GitHub Actions cron + Slack notification) to prevent calendar drift
Maturity levels: tabletop → guided → unguided → production → chaos; match level to runbook maturity
